content:
# Genesis Chapter 32 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 18v–19r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_032_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_032_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 29–30 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0033 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0034

---

*Jacob Encounters Angels at Mahanaim (vv. 1–2)*

**32:1** And Jacob went [וַיֵּלֶךְ יַעֲקֹב] on his way [לְדַרְכּוֹ] — and the angels of God [וַיִּפְגְּעוּ-בוֹ מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים] met him [וַיִּפְגְּעוּ בוֹ].

**32:2** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] when he saw them [כַּאֲשֶׁר רָאָם]: This is [זֶה] the camp of God [מַחֲנֵה אֱלֹהִים] — and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] the name of [שֵׁם] that place [הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא] Mahanaim [מַחֲנָיִם].

---

*Jacob Sends Messengers to Esau (vv. 3–5)*

**32:3** And Jacob sent [וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב] messengers [מַלְאָכִים] before him [לְפָנָיו] to Esau [אֶל-עֵשָׂו] his brother [אָחִיו] — to the land of Seir [אַרְצָה שֵׂעִיר] the field of Edom [שְׂדֵה אֱדוֹם].

**32:4** And he commanded [וַיְצַו] them [אֹתָם] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Thus you shall say [כֹּה תֹאמְרוּן] to my lord [לַאדֹנִי] to Esau [לְעֵשָׂו]: Thus says [כֹּה אָמַר] your servant [עַבְדְּךָ] Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] — I have sojourned [גַּרְתִּי] with Laban [עִם-לָבָן] and stayed [וָאֵחַר] until now [עַד-עָתָּה].

**32:5** And there were to me [וַיְהִי-לִי] oxen [שׁוֹר] and donkeys [וַחֲמוֹר] — flocks [צֹאן] and male-servant [וְעֶבֶד] and female-servant [וְשִׁפְחָה] — and I send [וָאֶשְׁלְחָה] to tell [לְהַגִּיד] my lord [לַאדֹנִי] — to find [לִמְצֹא] favor [חֵן] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֶיךָ].

---

*The Messengers Return — Esau Is Coming with Four Hundred Men (vv. 6–8)*

**32:6** And the messengers returned [וַיָּשׁוּבוּ הַמַּלְאָכִים] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב] saying [לֵאמֹר]: We came [בָּאנוּ] to your brother [אֶל-אָחִיךָ] to Esau [אֶל-עֵשָׂו] — and also [וְגַם] he is going [הֹלֵךְ] to meet you [לִקְרָאתְךָ] — and four hundred men [וְאַרְבַּע-מֵאוֹת אִישׁ] with him [עִמּוֹ].

**32:7** And Jacob was greatly afraid [וַיִּירָא יַעֲקֹב מְאֹד] and he was distressed [וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ] — and he divided [וַיַּחַץ] the people [אֶת-הָעָם] that was with him [אֲשֶׁר-אִתּוֹ] — and the flocks [וְאֶת-הַצֹּאן] and the cattle [וְאֶת-הַבָּקָר] and the camels [וְאֶת-הַגְּמַלִּים] — into two camps [לִשְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת].

**32:8** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: If Esau comes [אִם-יָבוֹא עֵשָׂו] to the one camp [אֶל-הַמַּחֲנֶה הָאַחַת] and strikes it [וְהִכָּהוּ] — then the camp [וְהָיָה הַמַּחֲנֶה] that remains [הַנִּשְׁאָר] shall be as an escaped remnant [לִפְלֵיטָה].

---

*Jacob's Prayer (vv. 9–12)*

**32:9** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב]: O God of my father Abraham [אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אַבְרָהָם] and God of my father Isaac [וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי יִצְחָק] — O LORD [יְהוָה] — who said [הָאֹמֵר] to me [אֵלַי]: Return [שׁוּב] to your land [לְאַרְצְךָ] and to your kindred [וּלְמוֹלַדְתֶּךָ] — and I will do good with you [וְאֵיטִיבָה עִמָּךְ].

**32:10** I am too small for [קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל] all the steadfast-loves [הַחֲסָדִים] and from all the faithfulness [וּמִכָּל-הָאֱמֶת] that you have done [אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ] with your servant [עִם-עַבְדֶּךָ] — for with my staff alone [כִּי בְמַקְלִי] I crossed [עָבַרְתִּי] this Jordan [אֶת-הַיַּרְדֵּן הַזֶּה] and now [וְעַתָּה] I have become two camps [הָיִיתִי לִשְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת].

**32:11** Deliver me [הַצִּילֵנִי נָא] — I pray [נָא] — from the hand of [מִיַּד] my brother [אָחִי] — from the hand of [מִיַּד] Esau [עֵשָׂו] — for I fear him [כִּי-יָרֵא אָנֹכִי אֹתוֹ] — lest he come [פֶּן-יָבוֹא] and strike me [וְהִכַּנִי] — the mother upon the children [אֵם עַל-בָּנִים].

**32:12** And you [וְאַתָּה] said [אָמַרְתָּ]: Doing-good I will do good [הֵיטֵב אֵיטִיב] with you [עִמָּךְ] — and I will make your seed [וְשַׂמְתִּי אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ] as the sand of the sea [כְּחוֹל הַיָּם] that cannot be counted [אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יִסָּפֵר] for abundance [מֵרֹב].

---

*The Gift-Caravan Prepared — Sent Before Jacob (vv. 13–21)*

**32:13** And he stayed the night [וַיָּלֶן שָׁם] there [שָׁם] on that night [בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא] — and he took [וַיִּקַּח] from what came to his hand [מִן-הַבָּא בְיָדוֹ] a gift [מִנְחָה] for Esau [לְעֵשָׂו] his brother [אָחִיו]:

**32:14** two hundred she-goats [עִזִּים מָאתַיִם] and twenty male-goats [וּתְיָשִׁים עֶשְׂרִים] — two hundred ewes [רְחֵלִים מָאתַיִם] and twenty rams [וְאֵילִים עֶשְׂרִים] —

**32:15** thirty nursing-camels [גְּמַלִּים מֵינִיקוֹת שְׁלֹשִׁים] and their colts [וּבְנֵיהֶם] — forty cows [פָּרוֹת אַרְבָּעִים] and ten bulls [וּפָרִים עֲשָׂרָה] — twenty female-donkeys [אֲתֹנֹת עֶשְׂרִים] and ten male-donkeys [וַעְיָרִם עֲשָׂרָה].

**32:16** And he put [וַיִּתֵּן] [them] into the hand of [בְּיַד] his servants [עֲבָדָיו] — each drove [עֵדֶר] by itself [לְבַדּוֹ] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to his servants [אֶל-עֲבָדָיו]: Pass over [עִבְרוּ] before me [לְפָנַי] and put [וְשִׂימוּ] a space [רֶוַח] between drove [בֵּין עֵדֶר] and drove [וּבֵין עֵדֶר].

**32:17** And he commanded [וַיְצַו] the first [אֶת-הָרִאשׁוֹן] saying [לֵאמֹר]: When Esau my brother [כִּי יִפְגָּשְׁךָ עֵשָׂו אָחִי] meets you [יִפְגָּשְׁךָ] and asks you [וּשְׁאֵלְךָ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: To whom do you belong [לְמִי-אַתָּה] and where are you going [וְאָנָה תֵלֵךְ] and whose [וּלְמִי] are these before you [אֵלֶּה לְפָנֶיךָ]?

**32:18** Then you shall say [וְאָמַרְתָּ]: To your servant [לְעַבְדְּךָ] to Jacob [לְיַעֲקֹב] — it is a gift [הִיא מִנְחָה] sent [שְׁלוּחָה] to my lord [לַאדֹנִי] to Esau [לְעֵשָׂו] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] also he [גַם-הוּא] is behind us [אַחֲרֵינוּ].

**32:19** And he commanded [וַיְצַו] also [גַּם] the second [אֶת-הַשֵּׁנִי] — also [גַּם] the third [אֶת-הַשְּׁלִישִׁי] — also all the ones going after [גַּם אֶת-כָּל-הַהֹלְכִים] the droves [אַחֲרֵי הָעֲדָרִים] saying [לֵאמֹר]: According to this word [כַּדָּבָר הַזֶּה] you shall speak [תְּדַבְּרוּן] to Esau [אֶל-עֵשָׂו] when you find him [בְּמֹצַאֲכֶם אֹתוֹ].

**32:20** And you shall also say [וַאֲמַרְתֶּם גַּם-הִנֵּה]: Behold also [גַּם-הִנֵּה] your servant Jacob [עַבְדְּךָ יַעֲקֹב] is behind us [אַחֲרֵינוּ] — for [כִּי] he said [אָמַר]: Let me appease his face [אֲכַפְּרָה פָנָיו] with the gift [בַּמִּנְחָה] that goes [הַהֹלֶכֶת] before me [לְפָנָי] — and afterward [וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן] I will see his face [אֶרְאֶה פָנָיו] — perhaps [אוּלַי] he will lift up my face [יִשָּׂא פָנָי].

**32:21** And the gift [וַתַּעֲבֹר הַמִּנְחָה] passed over [עָבַר] before him [עַל-פָּנָיו] — and he himself [וְהוּא] lodged [לָן] that night [בַּלַּיְלָה הַהוּא] in the camp [בַּמַּחֲנֶה].

---

*Jacob Crosses the Jabbok — He Sends His Family Over (vv. 22–23)*

**32:22** And he rose [וַיָּקָם] in that night [בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא] and took [וַיִּקַּח] his two wives [אֶת-שְׁתֵּי נָשָׁיו] and his two female-servants [וְאֶת-שְׁתֵּי שִׁפְחֹתָיו] and his eleven children [וְאֶת-אַחַד עָשָׂר יְלָדָיו] and crossed [וַיַּעֲבֹר] the ford of [אֵת מַעֲבַר] the Jabbok [יַבֹּק].

**32:23** And he took them [וַיִּקָּחֵם] and sent them over [וַיַּעֲבִרֵם] the stream [אֶת-הַנָּחַל] — and he sent over [וַיַּעֲבֵר] what was his [אֶת-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ].

---

*The Man Wrestles with Jacob Until Dawn (vv. 24–26)*

**32:24** And Jacob [וַיִּוָּתֵר יַעֲקֹב] was left alone [לְבַדּוֹ] — and a man [וַיֵּאָבֵק אִישׁ] wrestled with him [עִמּוֹ] until [עַד] the going up of the dawn [עֲלוֹת הַשָּׁחַר].

**32:25** And he saw [וַיַּרְא] that he had not prevailed over him [כִּי לֹא יָכֹל לוֹ] — and he touched [וַיִּגַּע] the socket of [בְּכַף] his thigh [יְרֵכוֹ] — and the socket of [וַתֵּקַע כַּף-יֶרֶךְ] Jacob's thigh [יַעֲקֹב] was dislocated [וַתֵּקַע] when he wrestled with him [בְּהֵאָבְקוֹ עִמּוֹ].

**32:26** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Send me away [שַׁלְּחֵנִי] — for the dawn has come up [כִּי עָלָה הַשָּׁחַר] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: I will not send you away [לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ] unless [כִּי אִם-] you have blessed me [בֵּרַכְתָּנִי].

---

*The Name Changed — Israel (vv. 27–29)*

**32:27** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to him [אֵלָיו]: What [מַה] is your name [שְׁמֶךָ]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Jacob [יַעֲקֹב].

**32:28** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Your name shall no longer be called [לֹא יַעֲקֹב יֵאָמֵר שִׁמְךָ] Jacob [יַעֲקֹב] — but rather [כִּי אִם-] Israel [יִשְׂרָאֵל] — for you have striven [כִּי-שָׂרִיתָ] with God [עִם-אֱלֹהִים] and with men [וְעִם-אֲנָשִׁים] and have prevailed [וַתּוּכָל].

**32:29** And Jacob asked [וַיִּשְׁאַל יַעֲקֹב] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Tell [הַגִּידָה-נָּא] me please [נָא] your name [שְׁמֶךָ] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Why [לָמָּה] is this [זֶה] — you ask [תִּשְׁאַל] my name [לִשְׁמִי]? And he blessed him [וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ] there [שָׁם].

---

*The Naming of Peniel (vv. 30–31)*

**32:30** And Jacob called [וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב] the name of [שֵׁם] the place [הַמָּקוֹם] Peniel [פְּנִיאֵל] — for [כִּי] I have seen [רָאִיתִי] God [אֱלֹהִים] face to face [פָּנִים אֶל-פָּנִים] — and my life [וַתִּנָּצֵל נַפְשִׁי] was delivered [וַתִּנָּצֵל].

**32:31** And the sun [וַיִּזְרַח-לוֹ הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ] rose upon him [זָרַח לוֹ] as he passed over [כַּאֲשֶׁר עָבַר] Penuel [אֶת-פְּנוּאֵל] — and he was limping [וְהוּא צֹלֵעַ] upon his thigh [עַל-יְרֵכוֹ].

---

*The Dietary Prohibition — The Sinew of the Hip Socket (vv. 32–33)*

**32:32** Therefore [עַל-כֵּן] the sons of Israel [לֹא-יֹאכְלוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל] do not eat [לֹא יֹאכְלוּ] the sinew of the hip-socket [אֶת-גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה] that is [אֲשֶׁר] on the socket of the thigh [עַל-כַּף הַיָּרֵךְ] until this day [עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה] — because [כִּי] he touched [נָגַע] the socket of [בְּכַף-יֶרֶךְ] Jacob's thigh [יַעֲקֹב] — in the sinew of the hip-socket [בְּגִיד הַנָּשֶׁה].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 18v–19r:**

LC page 1 (GEN_032_page_001.jpg) is photographed inverted (rotated 180 degrees), consistent with the West Semitic Research Project's archiving pattern for this folio series noted previously for folios in this sequence. Once re-oriented, the page is folio 18v and carries the conclusion of Genesis 31 (Laban's departure, the Gal-ed covenant) in the lower section and then opens Genesis 32 running upward through the columns from the new chapter marker. The three-column Sephardic square script is fully vocalized with Tiberian pointing and cantillation throughout. I can read the chapter opening clearly: the Mahanaim pericope (vv. 1–2, with מַחֲנָיִם distinctly legible), the messenger-dispatch to Esau (vv. 3–5, with אַרְצָה שֵׂעִיר שְׂדֵה אֱדוֹם), the return report and the division into two camps (vv. 6–8, with שְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת echoing the Mahanaim name), Jacob's prayer (vv. 9–12, with the characteristic קָטֹנְתִּי and the promise of seed as sand of the sea), and the gift-caravan list beginning in v. 13 with the detailed enumeration of animals. The parasha marker is visible at the chapter break. The name יְהוָה (YHWH) is clearly present in v. 9 with its Qere vocalization pointing. The word מִנְחָה (gift/tribute/offering) appears three times across vv. 13–21.

LC page 2 (GEN_032_page_002.jpg) is in standard orientation. This is folio 19r. It carries the wrestling narrative from approximately verse 22 through verse 33 and into the opening verses of Genesis 33. The three-column layout is fully legible. Key readings confirmed: יַבֹּק (Jabbok, v. 22) with its telltale word-sequence positioning; וַיֵּאָבֵק (wrestled/wrestle, v. 24) — the rare Nithpa'el verb form is clear; כַּף-יְרֵכוֹ (socket of his thigh, v. 25) visible with the dagesh in כַּף; the climactic name-change in v. 28 — יִשְׂרָאֵל — is visible; פְּנִיאֵל (v. 30) and the variant spelling פְּנוּאֵל (v. 31) are both legible; גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (the sinew of the hip socket) in vv. 32–33 is clear; and the final notation צֹלֵעַ (limping) is present. The chapter closes and Genesis 33 opens with Jacob lifting his eyes and seeing Esau. The masoretic parasha markers in the margins are visible for this section.

---

**32:1 — Mahanaim (*Machanayim*, מַחֲנָיִם) — "Two Camps":**
The place-name מַחֲנָיִם (*Machanayim*) is the dual form of מַחֲנֶה (*machaneh*, "camp, encampment"). The dual suffix -ayim (יִם-) typically indicates either a literal pair or a place characterized by pairing/duality: "Two Camps." The narrative rationale is given explicitly in v. 2 — Jacob names the place because the angels met him in a camp that mirrored (or complemented) his own. The Mahanaim naming functions as a thematic hinge: the "two camps" of the divine assembly at the border anticipate the "two camps" into which Jacob divides his own household in v. 7 (שְׁנֵי מַחֲנוֹת, using the plural construct of the same noun), and both in turn recall the double camp — Jacob's earthly caravan and the heavenly escort. The narrator draws a deliberate structural parallel: Jacob re-enters the land guarded by two forces, and he splits into two forces precisely because he fears Esau. The place Mahanaim is historically locatable in Transjordan (cf. 2 Samuel 2:8, 12, 29 — Ish-bosheth's capital; 2 Samuel 17:24 — David's refuge from Absalom) and lay near the Jabbok, setting the stage for the wrestling.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *Parembolē Theou* (Παρεμβολὴ Θεοῦ) — "Camp of God" — for Jacob's exclamation in v. 2, then uses *Manaaim* (Μαναάμ) for the transliteration of the place-name — a Greek phonetic rendering of the Hebrew dual.

---

**32:1 — "Messengers/Angels" (*mal'akhim*, מַלְאָכִים) — The Same Word as 32:3:**
The Hebrew word מַלְאָכִים (*mal'akhim*) means both "angels" (divine messengers) and "messengers" (human envoys) — there is no lexical distinction in the Hebrew. The word used for the divine beings who meet Jacob in v. 1 is identical to the word used for the human emissaries Jacob sends to Esau in v. 3. This is not accidental. The narrative establishes a mirroring structure: Jacob is met by divine *mal'akhim* at the border of the land (v. 1); immediately Jacob dispatches human *mal'akhim* to Esau (v. 3). The ambiguity is theologically productive — Jacob moves from the divine realm to the human realm, from angelic encounter to political calculation. EMET renders v. 1 as "angels" and v. 3 as "messengers" following the contextual distinction, but notes that both are the same Hebrew word. This wordplay is visible on the LC manuscript page where the same consonantal string מלאכים appears in both verses. The LXX Vaticanus uses *angeloi* (ἄγγελοι) for both — the same word in Greek as in English, which retains the identical ambiguity.

---

**32:3 — "The land of Seir, the field of Edom" (*artzah Se'ir sedeh Edom*, אַרְצָה שֵׂעִיר שְׂדֵה אֱדוֹם):**
Two geographical designations are placed in apposition: the land of Seir (the mountain range of the Edomite heartland, south and east of the Dead Sea) and the field/country of Edom (the territory of the Edomite nation). Seir and Edom are frequently paired (cf. Genesis 36:8–9; Numbers 24:18; Deuteronomy 2:4–5; Judges 5:4). "Field" (שָׂדֶה, *sadeh*) in this context means "territory, country, open land" — not a single agricultural field but the open terrain of a region. The verse thus locates Esau precisely in his ancestral territory and anticipates the entire Esau-Edom identification developed in Genesis 36.

---

**32:4 — "My lord" (*'adonî*, אֲדֹנִי) and "your servant" (*'avdekha*, עַבְדְּךָ) — Diplomatic Formula:**
Jacob instructs his messengers to address Esau as "my lord" (אֲדֹנִי) and refer to Jacob himself as "your servant" (עַבְדְּךָ). This is the standard ancient Near Eastern diplomatic formula of subordination — the inferior addresses the superior with these titles. The choice is deliberate and loaded: Jacob was the one who took the birthright and the blessing, wresting the status of firstborn from Esau by deception. Now he addresses Esau as *lord*. The words echo the blessing of Isaac in Genesis 27:29 — "Let peoples serve you and nations bow down to you; be lord over your brothers, and let your mother's sons bow down to you." Jacob had taken that blessing, but now he enacts the posture of the one who bows. The LXX Vaticanus reads *tō kyriō mou Ēsau* (τῷ κυρίῳ μου Ἠσαῦ) — "to my lord Esau" — exact rendering.

---

**32:7 — "Greatly afraid and distressed" (*wayyira' Ya'aqov me'od wayyetser lo*, וַיִּירָא יַעֲקֹב מְאֹד וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ):**
Two distinct emotional states are indicated. The first — יִרְאָה (*yir'ah*, "fear") — is the common word for fear or reverence. The second — וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ (*wayyetser lo*) — is literally "and it was narrow/confined to him," from the root צוּר/צָרַר (*tsur/tsarar*, "to be narrow, to press, to bind"). The construction יָצַר לוֹ means "it was distressing to him / he was in straits." The pairing of the two terms — fear and anguish — captures the full intensity of Jacob's psychological state. EMET renders them sequentially. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ēporēthē de Iakōb* (ἠπορήθη δε Ἰακώβ) — "and Jacob was perplexed/at a loss" — which translates the nuance of being in straits but loses the dual structure of the MT.

---

**32:8 — "The camp that remains shall be as an escaped remnant" (*liphletah*, לִפְלֵיטָה):**
The word פְּלֵיטָה (*pĕletah*, "escape, remnant of survivors") comes from the root פָּלַט (*palat*, "to escape, to deliver"). It is often used in contexts of military defeat and survival — the remnant that escaped the sword (cf. Ezra 9:13–14; Nehemiah 1:2; 2 Kings 19:30–31). Jacob's tactical plan — splitting into two camps so one might survive — reveals his realistic assessment of the mortal danger. The word *pĕletah* also carries theological resonance: in the prophetic tradition, the surviving remnant (*she'erit* and *pĕletah*) are the locus of God's continued redemptive purposes. Jacob, unwittingly, frames his contingency plan in the language of eschatological survival. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eis sōtērian* (εἰς σωτηρίαν) — "for salvation/deliverance" — a theologically charged rendering.

---

**32:10 — "I am too small for all the steadfast-loves" (*qatonti mikkol ha-chasadim*, קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל הַחֲסָדִים):**
This verse contains one of the most rhetorically compressed confessions of unworthiness in the Hebrew Bible. The verb קָטֹן (*qaton*, "to be small, to be too little") in the perfect first-person — קָטֹנְתִּי (*qatonti*) — is used comparatively with מִן (*min*, "from/than"): "I am too small than / I have become too small for..." The noun חֲסָדִים (*chasadim*) is the plural of חֶסֶד (*chesed*, "steadfast love, covenant loyalty, loving-kindness") — one of the most theologically rich words in the Hebrew Bible. The plural may intensify or enumerate: "all the acts of steadfast-love / all the loyal-kindnesses." Jacob is saying: I do not deserve what you have done for me. The pairing with אֱמֶת (*'emet*, "faithfulness, truth") — the other great covenantal term — creates the classic biblical word-pair *chesed we-'emet* ("steadfast love and faithfulness"), appearing here in reverse order and in plural. EMET renders *chasadim* as "steadfast-loves" (hyphenated plural) to preserve both the plural and the semantic weight of the covenantal root.

The second half of the verse — "for with my staff alone I crossed this Jordan and now I have become two camps" — creates a powerful contrast: he crossed with nothing but a walking-stick; he returns with a substantial household and enough wealth to organize into two armed camps. The Jordan here functions as the border he had crossed when fleeing (Genesis 31:21 mentions crossing "the River" — the Euphrates — in fleeing Laban; but Jacob originally crossed the Jordan when fleeing to Laban in Genesis 28. Here he refers back to the original crossing with nothing). The LXX Vaticanus reads *hikanei moi* (ἱκανεῖ μοι) — "sufficient for me / I am sufficient for" — but this is actually the opposite sense from the MT's declaration of insufficiency. This is a noteworthy LXX variant: where the MT says "I am too small/unworthy for," the LXX says "I am adequate/satisfied with." The LXX may have read קָטֹנְתִּי differently or derived from a different verbal root, but the MT reading — as a confession of unworthiness — is textually secure and contextually superior, fitting the structure of a prayer of humble petition.

---

**32:20 — "Let me appease his face" (*'akhapperah fanaw*, אֲכַפְּרָה פָנָיו) — Atonement Language:**
The verb כִּפֶּר (*kipper*, Pi'el of כָּפַר) is the same root that underlies the noun כַּפֹּרֶת (*kapporet*, "the atonement-cover / mercy seat") and the entire system of atonement in the Levitical sacrificial code. The verb means "to cover over, to make atonement, to pacify, to appease." Jacob uses sacrificial/atoning language to describe sending the gift ahead — he will "cover the face of" Esau, i.e., divert Esau's wrathful gaze with an appeasing offering. The "face" (*panim*, פָּנִים) is a recurrent word in this chapter: Jacob seeks to appease Esau's face (v. 20), hopes Esau will "lift up" his face (v. 20), and later names the site of the wrestling Peniel — "face of God" — because he has seen God face to face (v. 30). The face (*panim*) is the locus of both wrath and grace, both hostility and blessing, throughout the Jacob-Esau-divine sequence. The Hebrew idiom "to lift up the face" (שָׂא פָנִים, v. 20) means to show favor, accept, or be gracious — the petitioner hopes Esau will receive Jacob graciously rather than turn his face against him. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hilaskomai to prosōpon autou* (ἱλάσκομαι τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ) — "I will propitiate his face" — preserving the cultic/atoning nuance of *kipper*.

---

**32:22–24 — The Triple Wordplay: Ya'aqov / Yabboq / ye'aveq (יַעֲקֹב / יַבֹּק / וַיֵּאָבֵק):**
The three verses that open the wrestling pericope contain one of the most celebrated pieces of Hebrew wordplay in the entire Bible. The three words share the same three consonantal root elements — y-q-b / y-b-q / '-v-q — in near-anagrammatic arrangement:

1. **יַעֲקֹב** (*Ya'aqov*, "Jacob") — y-'-q-b — the patriarch's name
2. **יַבֹּק** (*Yabboq*, "Jabbok") — y-b-q — the river name
3. **וַיֵּאָבֵק** (*wayyê'āveq*, "and he wrestled") — '-v-q — the verb for wrestling

The consonants y, q, and b (ב) / v (cognate with ב) are rearranged across all three. The river Jabbok (today's Nahr az-Zarqa, "Blue River," in Jordan) gives its name to the wrestling — the very geography is encoded in the action. And Jacob's name — built on the root עָקַב (*'āqav*, "to grasp the heel, to follow at the heel, to circumvent/supplant") — stands in phonetic tension with both the place and the deed. EMET cannot reproduce this wordplay in English, but notes it here in full. The river name and the verb for wrestling are etymologically related (*Jabbok* from the same root as *wrestle* in the verb *'āveq* [אָבַק], literally "to kick up dust, to grapple"), and both echo the name of the man who grapples. On the LC manuscript page this wordplay is visible to the eye of the scholar reading: יַעֲקֹב ... יַבֹּק ... וַיֵּאָבֵק — the three words in immediate proximity.

---

**32:24 — "A man wrestled with him" (*'ish*, אִישׁ) — Who Is the 'ish?:**
The assailant is introduced simply as אִישׁ (*'ish*, "a man"). This is one of the most deliberate acts of narrative reticence in the Hebrew Bible. The text never identifies the 'ish directly. The interpretive tradition is rich and divided:

1. **An angel** — Hosea 12:4–5 retrospectively interprets the encounter: "he strove with an angel and prevailed... he met God at Bethel." The Hosea passage uses both "angel" (*mal'akh*) and "God" (*Elohim*) for the same encounter, suggesting the tradition understood the being as an angelic representative of God.

2. **God himself** — Jacob's own naming at Peniel in v. 30 — "I have seen *God* face to face" — suggests Jacob believed he had wrestled with the divine directly. The name Israel in v. 28 — "you have striven with *God*" (עִם-אֱלֹהִים, *'im-Elohim*) — confirms this reading.

3. **The divine guardian of Esau / an Edomite deity** — Some critical scholars propose the 'ish is a tutelary spirit or night-demon guarding the river crossing, in the tradition of river-ford guardian spirits attested across ancient Near Eastern literature.

4. **Jacob's own inner self / Esau** — Allegorical and psychological readings abound in both Jewish and Christian tradition.

EMET translates *'ish* as "a man" — the precise, literal word — and declines to resolve the identity in the translation itself. The ambiguity is the text's own and must not be smoothed away by editorial choice. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai epalaien anthrōpos met' autou* (καὶ ἐπάλαιεν ἄνθρωπος μετ' αὐτοῦ) — "and a man wrestled with him" — *anthrōpos* being the Greek equivalent of *'ish/adam*, a generic human being. The LXX likewise preserves the ambiguity.

---

**32:24 — "Until the going up of the dawn" (*'ad 'alot ha-shakhar*, עַד עֲלוֹת הַשָּׁחַר):**
The phrase עֲלוֹת הַשַּׁחַר (*'alot ha-shakhar*) — "the going up / the rising of the dawn" — uses the infinitive construct עֲלוֹת (*'alot*, from עָלָה, "to go up, to ascend") with the definite article on הַשַּׁחַר (*ha-shakhar*, "the dawn"). The dawn *rises* in Hebrew — it ascends, it comes up. The fight lasts the entire night. The same word *shakhar* ("dawn") appears in the Song of Songs 6:10 and in Psalm 139:9, always with the sense of the first light ascending before the full sunrise. The wrestling ends at dawn — but the sun does not fully rise until v. 31, when Jacob passes over Penuel and *the sun shone on him*. The progression from darkness through dawn to sunrise marks the passage from the nocturnal wrestling encounter to the daylit world of human affairs (and Esau's approach in Genesis 33).

---

**32:25 — "The socket of his thigh" (*kaf-yereko*, כַּף-יְרֵכוֹ) — Anatomy and Translation:**
The term כַּף-יָרֵךְ (*kaf-yarekh*) is anatomically precise: כַּף (*kaf*) means "palm, hollow, socket" — the concave receiving-joint — and יָרֵךְ (*yarekh*) means "thigh, upper leg" — but with a semantic range that also includes the loins/hip region (it is the same word used for thigh-swearing in Genesis 24:2, 9 and 47:29). Together, כַּף-יָרֵךְ is the hip socket — the acetabulum, the hollow cup of the pelvis that receives the femoral head. The touch (*naga'*, נָגַע — used later also for the *gid ha-nasheh* verb in v. 32) causes the joint to be dislocated (*watteqa'*, וַתֵּקַע — from יָקַע or תָּקַע, "to be thrust out, dislocated, displaced"). The resulting injury — a dislocated hip — is the sign carried by Jacob/Israel into the daylit world and celebrated in the dietary law of v. 32. EMET renders *kaf-yerekho* as "the socket of his thigh" throughout, noting that "thigh socket" or "hip socket" captures the anatomical sense more precisely than simply "thigh." The LXX Vaticanus reads *epi to platos tou mērou autou* (ἐπὶ τὸ πλάτος τοῦ μηροῦ αὐτοῦ) — "upon the breadth/width of his thigh" — *platos tou mērou* being a general term for the hip/thigh region rather than the specific socket.

---

**32:28 — Israel (*Yisra'el*, יִשְׂרָאֵל) — The Etymology:**
The name יִשְׂרָאֵל (*Yisra'el*) is explained by the 'ish in v. 28 with a ki-clause: כִּי-שָׂרִיתָ עִם-אֱלֹהִים וְעִם-אֲנָשִׁים וַתּוּכָל — "for you have striven with God and with men and have prevailed." The explanation derives the name from the verb שָׂרָה (*sarah*, "to strive, to contend, to persist, to have power") + אֵל (*El*, "God") — thus "he who strives with God" or "God strives/persists." The verbal root שָׂרָה is rare; it appears in v. 28 and in Hosea 12:3–4 in explicit reference to this event, and possibly in Genesis 32:25 (כִּי לֹא יָכֹל לוֹ, "for he could not prevail over him" — the root יָכֹל being related to the overcoming/prevailing language of the etymology). The name is given by the divine being who cannot (or will not) reveal his own name (v. 29). This is structurally parallel to Genesis 17:5 (Abraham) and 17:15 (Sarah) — the divine name-change as covenant identity-marker.

The alternate reading of the name as "God strives" (taking El as the subject of *sarah*) would mean: "God strives/contends/persists [on Israel's behalf]." Both readings of the theophoric element — God as object (Jacob strives with God) and God as subject (God strives for Jacob) — are possible within the grammar, and the ambiguity may be intentional. The name Israel thus carries within it the entire tension of the Jacob-divine encounter: the patriarch who prevails over God by clinging and demanding blessing, and the God who prevails by marking and renaming the patriarch. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Israēl* (Ἰσραήλ) — transliterating — and gives the etymological gloss *hoti ēnischysas meta Theou kai meta anthrōpōn dynatos* (ὅτι ἐνίσχυσας μετὰ Θεοῦ καὶ μετὰ ἀνθρώπων δυνατός) — "because you were strong with God and with men, being powerful" — using *ēnischysas* (from *ischys*, "strength") rather than a word for striving/contending. The LXX has smoothed the etymology toward "strength" rather than "striving."

---

**32:29 — "Why do you ask my name?" — The Divine Name Withheld:**
The refusal to answer Jacob's question "What is your name?" — וַיֹּאמֶר לָמָּה זֶּה תִּשְׁאַל לִשְׁמִי (*wayyomer lamah zeh tish'al lishmi*, "and he said: Why — this — do you ask for my name?") — is one of the most significant moments of divine reticence in the Hebrew Bible. The pattern of the divine name being withheld or unknown recurs: Judges 13:17–18 (Manoah asks the angel "What is your name?" — the angel replies, "Why do you ask my name? It is *peli'i* [wonderful / too wonderful]") — the parallel is exact. The withholding of the divine name establishes the asymmetry of the encounter: Jacob knows who he wrestled with (he names the place "Face of God"), but he does not receive the Name. The divine being retains an irreducible mystery. This reticence is itself a form of revelation — you cannot command or conjure what you cannot name. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hina ti touto erōtas to onoma mou* (ἵνα τί τοῦτο ἐρωτᾷς τὸ ὄνομά μου) — "for what reason do you ask this, my name?" — an exact rendering of the Hebrew interrogative construction.

---

**32:30 — Peniel / Penuel (*Pĕni'el* / *Pĕnu'el*, פְּנִיאֵל / פְּנוּאֵל) — Face of God:**
The place is called פְּנִיאֵל (*Pĕni'el*) in v. 30 — the name as given by Jacob in the speech — and פְּנוּאֵל (*Pĕnu'el*) in v. 31 — the toponym as the narrator uses it in the geographical/narrative description. Both forms mean "Face of God" — פְּנֵי + אֵל (*pĕnei El*) — "faces of God" — the construct plural פְּנֵי being the standard form for "face" in construct (Hebrew regularly uses the plural for anatomically paired features: פָּנִים, *panim*, "face," is always grammatically plural). The dual spelling — Peniel (theophanic naming) and Penuel (geographical toponym) — preserves the word's two registers: the theological declaration of the moment and the real-world place-name. Penuel appears later in the historical narratives as an actual fortified site in Transjordan (Judges 8:8–9, 17 — Gideon's conflict with Penuel; 1 Kings 12:25 — Jeroboam builds up Penuel).

The naming formula — כִּי-רָאִיתִי אֱלֹהִים פָּנִים אֶל-פָּנִים וַתִּנָּצֵל נַפְשִׁי (*ki-ra'iti Elohim panim el-panim wattinnatsel nafshi*, "for I have seen God face to face and my life was delivered") — articulates an ancient theological conviction: to see God is to die (cf. Exodus 33:20 — "no one can see my face and live"). Jacob's naming of the place expresses astonishment at survival: he has done the impossible thing and lived. The verb נִצַּל (*nittsal*, Niphal of נָצַל, "to be delivered, to be snatched away, to escape") is used — נַפְשִׁי וַתִּנָּצֵל — "my life was delivered/snatched away [from death]." The LXX Vaticanus reads *eidos Theon prosōpon pros prosōpon* (εἶδος Θεὸν πρόσωπον πρὸς πρόσωπον) — "I saw God face to face" — and *kai esōthē mou hē psychē* (καὶ ἐσώθη μου ἡ ψυχή) — "and my life was saved" — using *sōthē* (from *sōzō*, "to save") for the MT's *wattinnatsĕl*.

---

**32:31 — "The sun rose upon him" (*wayyizrakh-lo ha-shemesh*, וַיִּזְרַח-לוֹ הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ) and "limping" (*tsolea'*, צֹלֵעַ):**
The phrase וַיִּזְרַח-לוֹ הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ — literally "and the sun rose/shone *for him* / *upon him*" — uses the lamed of advantage/reference (לוֹ, *lo*): the sun rose *for Jacob* specifically. This is not a neutral temporal marker but a loaded observation — the sun rises as Jacob emerges from the darkness of the wrestling, limping but blessed. The participle צֹלֵעַ (*tsolea'*, from צָלַע, "to limp, to go lamely") is the narrator's observation, not Jacob's self-description. He crosses over Penuel limping — his injury is permanent (or at least ongoing at this narrative point). The sun rising and the limping together form the closing image of the night episode: Jacob/Israel, wounded and blessed, moving into the light. The same verb צָלַע (*tsala'*) appears in Micah 4:6–7 for the gathering of the lame remnant. The LXX Vaticanus reads *anateilantos de autō tou hēliou* (ἀνατείλαντος δε αὐτῷ τοῦ ἡλίου) — "but the sun having risen for him" — and *chōlos tē ischyi autou* (χωλὸς τῇ ἰσχύι αὐτοῦ) — "lame in his strength/hip" — a somewhat interpretive rendering.

---

**32:32–33 — Gid ha-nasheh (*gid ha-nasheh*, גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה) — The Dietary Prohibition:**
The term גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (*gid ha-nasheh*) appears only here in the Hebrew Bible (vv. 32 and 33), making its precise anatomical identification a matter of philological inference and rabbinic discussion. גִּיד (*gid*) is a sinew, tendon, or cord (the same word used for bow-strings in Job 30:11 and Psalm 11:2, and for sinews in Ezekiel 37:6–8). הַנָּשֶׁה (*ha-nasheh*) is a participle from נָשָׁה (*nasheh*, "to be forgetful, to be displaced, to be moved") — some derive it from a root meaning "to slip away, to be dislocated." The combination — "the sinew of the displaced one / the sinew of forgetting" — refers to the sciatic nerve or the tendon of the hip, which was displaced when Jacob's hip socket was touched. The prohibition in v. 32 — "therefore the sons of Israel do not eat the gid ha-nasheh... until this day" — is the etiological explanation for an Israelite dietary practice. Rabbinic halakhah (Hullin 89b) identifies the gid ha-nasheh as the sciatic nerve and surrounding muscle tissue on the hip socket, and its removal (*nikkur* or *porging*) is a specialized butchery practice still observed in traditional kosher preparation. EMET transliterates the term as "the sinew of the hip-socket" in the translation and preserves *gid ha-nasheh* as the proper technical term in this note.

The narrative positions the dietary law as an embodied commemoration: every time Israel does not eat this sinew, they remember Jacob's wrestling, his injury, and his blessing. The law is inscribed not in a text but in the practice of the body — in what is not eaten. The LXX Vaticanus reads *to neuron to platos tou mērou* (τὸ νεῦρον τὸ πλάτος τοῦ μηροῦ) — "the sinew of the breadth of the thigh" — *neuron* being a Greek word for sinew, tendon, or nerve.

---

**Verse 33 — MT Versification Note:**
The Masoretic Text of Genesis 32 contains 33 verses. Verse 33 in the MT (the dietary prohibition conclusion — "because he had touched the socket of Jacob's thigh at the sinew of the hip-socket") is the concluding clause of the chapter. The LXX (Vaticanus) numbers this chapter with 32 verses due to the offset introduced by counting MT 31:55 as LXX 32:1 — the entire LXX chapter is thus shifted back one verse, with LXX Genesis 32 beginning with what MT numbers as 32:1 (Laban's departure) and running through the wrestling episode and dietary law as LXX 32:32. EMET follows the MT versification (33 verses) throughout.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 29–30):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 29–30 carry Genesis 32 across two pages in three-column uncial Greek without word-breaks or accents. The chapter margin notation on page 29 reads *CAP. XXXVI* — this is the ancient Greek chapter numbering, beginning with what the MT numbers as Genesis 31:55/32:1. Page 30 shows *CAP. XXXVII* beginning partway through the page, corresponding to the opening of Genesis 33 in the MT.

Key LXX variants for Genesis 32:

1. **32:2 (LXX 32:2)** — LXX *Parembolē Theou* (Παρεμβολὴ Θεοῦ) — "Camp of God" — for MT's *machaneh Elohim*. Then *Manaaim* (Μαναάμ) for the place-name Mahanaim. The Greek drops the dual form's sense of "two camps."

2. **32:10 (LXX 32:10)** — LXX *hikanei moi* (ἱκανεῖ μοι) — "it is sufficient for me" — for MT *qatonti mikkol ha-chasadim* — "I am too small for all the steadfast-loves." A significant divergence: the MT is a confession of unworthiness; the LXX reads as an acknowledgment of adequacy or sufficiency. The LXX may have read from a different Vorlage or misread the root *qatan* through the lens of *hikanoo* (to be sufficient/worthy). The MT reading is supported by the overall context of the prayer as a humble petition for deliverance.

3. **32:20 (LXX 32:20)** — LXX *hilaskomai to prosōpon autou* — "I will propitiate his face" — for MT *'akhapperah fanaw* — preserving the atoning/propitiatory sense of *kipper*.

4. **32:24 (LXX 32:24)** — LXX *anthrōpos* (ἄνθρωπος) — "a man" — for MT *'ish*. Both are generic terms for a human being; the LXX preserves the deliberate ambiguity.

5. **32:25 (LXX 32:25)** — LXX *epi to platos tou mērou autou* — "upon the breadth of his thigh" — for MT *kaf-yereko* — "the socket of his thigh." The LXX is anatomically less specific.

6. **32:28 (LXX 32:28)** — LXX *hoti ēnischysas meta Theou kai meta anthrōpōn dynatos* — "because you were strong with God and with men" — using *ēnischysas* (from *ischys*, "strength/force") rather than a striving/contending verb. The MT *sarita* implies wrestling/contending; the LXX shifts toward "being strong."

7. **32:29 (LXX 32:29)** — LXX *hina ti touto erōtas to onoma mou* — exact rendering of the refusal to give the name, preserving the reticence of the MT.

8. **32:30 (LXX 32:30)** — LXX *eidos Theon prosōpon pros prosōpon... kai esōthē mou hē psychē* — "I saw God face to face... and my life was saved." *Esōthē* (was saved) for MT *wattinnatsĕl* (was delivered/snatched). The LXX shifts from "snatched out of danger" to "saved."

9. **32:32 (LXX 32:32)** — LXX *to neuron to platos tou mērou* — "the sinew of the breadth of the thigh" — for MT *gid ha-nasheh*. The LXX renders rather than transliterates; the Greek term does not preserve the specific term *nasheh* (with its connotations of displacement/forgetting).

10. **LXX CAP. XXXVI / XXXVII** — The LXX chapter divisions (XXXVI and XXXVII on pages 29–30) do not correspond to the MT chapter divisions. CAP. XXXVI begins at MT 31:55 (LXX 32:1) and CAP. XXXVII begins at the opening of what the MT calls Genesis 33.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 32 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant for this chapter. The Leningrad Codex (primary Hebrew witness) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary Greek witness) are the two principal manuscript sources examined for this chapter.
